1:
"He that is wounded in the stones, or hath his privy member cut off, shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD." The very brief verse notes the exclusion of the maimed from the congregation. The grammatical voices note the exclusion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he that is wounded in the stones, or hath his privy member cut off, shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD" — the man maimed in this way, often by the heathen for idolatrous service, was excluded from the assembly; the he that is wounded in the stones, or hath his privy member cut off (the maimed); the shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD (the exclusion from the assembly); the figure (the exclusion of the maimed, often connected with heathen idolatry, from the holy assembly; the truth that the congregation of the LORD was to be kept pure, certain disqualifications barring entrance; the figure of the holiness required for access to God, the ceremonial bar a shadow of the purity God requires; the exclusion that points to the perfect access opened in Christ, where the eunuch is welcomed, "let not the eunuch say, Behold, I am a dry tree... I will give them an everlasting name," Isaiah 56:3-5; the ceremonial separation under the law fulfilled in the gospel's welcome, Acts 8:27-39). The verse holds together the exclusion of the maimed.

On "He that is wounded in the stones, or hath his privy member cut off," the witnesses note the maimed. Gill notes the man maimed, often by heathen idolatry (the disqualification) — the maimed (excluded).

On "shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the exclusion: "shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD" — the exclusion from the assembly (the purity of the congregation, Isaiah 56:3-5); the figure (the holiness required for access, fulfilled in Christ); the providence (the exclusion, the bastard and the Ammonite to follow, vv.2-3). The verse displays the exclusion of the maimed from the congregation.

2:
"A bastard shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD; even to his tenth generation shall he not enter into the congregation of the LORD." The very brief verse notes the exclusion of the bastard to the tenth generation. The grammatical voices note the exclusion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "a bastard shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD; even to his tenth generation shall he not enter" — the bastard, the child of incest or forbidden union, was barred from the assembly to the tenth generation; the a bastard shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD (the exclusion of the bastard); the even to his tenth generation shall he not enter into the congregation of the LORD (to the tenth generation); the figure (the exclusion of the bastard, a mark against unlawful unions, the purity of the assembly; the truth that the sin of unlawful union was visited in the exclusion of the offspring, a deterrent and a mark of God's holiness; the figure of the ceremonial separation that guarded the assembly's purity, fulfilled in the gospel where none who come to Christ are cast out; the tenth generation a figure of a long but not endless exclusion, the shadow giving way to grace; the holiness of the congregation guarded by the law, vv.1). The verse holds together the exclusion of the bastard.

The verse holds together the exclusion of Ammon and Moab.

5:
"Nevertheless the LORD thy God would not hearken unto Balaam; but the LORD thy God turned the curse into a blessing unto thee, because the LORD thy God loved thee." The witnesses note that God turned Balaam's curse into a blessing because he loved Israel — the crux of God's sovereign love that overrules the curses of the enemy. On "the LORD thy God turned the curse into a blessing unto thee, because the LORD thy God loved thee," interpreters expound the deliverance. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the curse turned — "the LORD thy God would not hearken unto Balaam; but the LORD thy God turned the curse into a blessing unto thee, because the LORD thy God loved thee": God would not let Balaam curse Israel, but turned the intended curse into blessing, for his love; the witnesses note the significance — the sovereign love of God that overrules the malice of the enemy (Balaam hired to curse, but God made him bless, the curse turned to blessing by God's hand); the lesson — that God's love guards his people, no curse can prevail against those he loves (the enemy's curse made void, turned to its opposite by God; the ground was not Israel's worth but God's love); the truth that God overrules the designs of the wicked for the good of his people, "there is no enchantment against Jacob, neither is there any divination against Israel," Numbers 23:23; the figure — the sovereign love and providence of God that makes the curse a blessing, "if God be for us, who can be against us?" Romans 8:31; the truth that all things, even the enemy's malice, work together for good to them that love God, "all things work together for good to them that love God," Romans 8:28; the figure of the believer guarded by God's love, no weapon formed against him prospering, "no weapon that is formed against thee shall prosper," Isaiah 54:17; the truth that God's electing love is the security of his people, the curse of the enemy turned to blessing; the warning to the enemies of God's people, that their curses recoil and become blessings to the elect. The figure of God's sovereign love that turns the curse into a blessing.

14:
"For the LORD thy God walketh in the midst of thy camp, to deliver thee, and to give up thine enemies before thee; therefore shall thy camp be holy: that he see no unclean thing in thee, and turn away from thee." The witnesses note that God walks in the midst of the camp, so it must be holy — the crux of the holiness required by the presence of God. On "the LORD thy God walketh in the midst of thy camp... therefore shall thy camp be holy," interpreters expound the reason. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the presence — "for the LORD thy God walketh in the midst of thy camp, to deliver thee... therefore shall thy camp be holy: that he see no unclean thing in thee, and turn away from thee": the camp was to be holy because God himself walked in it; the witnesses note the significance — the holiness required by the presence of God among his people (God present in the camp to deliver and give victory, his presence demanding purity); the lesson — that the presence of God requires holiness, nothing unclean to be tolerated where he dwells (the camp kept pure lest God see uncleanness and turn away; the reason of all the laws of the camp, God's presence); the truth that God dwells among his people and requires them to be holy, "be ye holy; for I am holy," 1 Peter 1:16; Leviticus 11:44; the figure — the church and the believer as the dwelling of God, called to holiness by his presence, "ye are the temple of the living God... wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate," 2 Corinthians 6:16-17; the truth that God's indwelling presence is the ground and motive of holiness, the body a temple of the Holy Ghost, "know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost?" 1 Corinthians 6:19; the figure of the holiness that God's presence demands, the warning that uncleanness drives him away; the truth that to keep God's presence the people must be holy, his holiness the pattern and his presence the motive. The figure of the holiness required by the indwelling presence of God.

15:
"Thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee:" The witnesses note the law that an escaped slave was not to be returned to his master — the crux of mercy to the fugitive and refuge for the oppressed. On "thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee," interpreters expound the law. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the refuge — "thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee": a slave who fled to Israel, especially from a foreign or cruel master, was not to be handed back; the witnesses note the significance — the mercy of the law toward the fugitive and oppressed (the escaped servant, likely fleeing a heathen or harsh master, given refuge, not returned to bondage); the lesson — that God's law provides asylum for the oppressed, the fugitive not to be betrayed (the slave who sought shelter to be received, not delivered up; the humanity of the law that shelters the weak against the powerful); the truth that God is the refuge of the oppressed, his law sheltering those who flee cruelty, "the LORD also will be a refuge for the oppressed," Psalm 9:9; the figure — the asylum that the gospel offers to all who flee to Christ, the refuge for the bond and the oppressed, "the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to preach deliverance to the captives... to set at liberty them that are bruised," Luke 4:18; the truth that God's heart is toward the fugitive and the oppressed, the law a shadow of his mercy; the figure of the refuge given to the one who flees from bondage, the door not shut against him; the contrast with the cruelty that would return the fleeing slave, the mercy that receives him. The figure of mercy to the fugitive and refuge for the oppressed.

19:
"Thou shalt not lend upon usury to thy brother; usury of money, usury of victuals, usury of any thing that is lent upon usury:" The very brief verse notes the prohibition of lending on usury to a brother. 20:
"Unto a stranger thou mayest lend upon usury; but unto thy brother thou shalt not lend upon usury: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all that thou settest thine hand to in the land whither thou goest to possess it." The very brief verse notes that usury might be charged a stranger but not a brother, with blessing on obedience. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "unto a stranger thou mayest lend upon usury; but unto thy brother thou shalt not lend upon usury: that the LORD thy God may bless thee" — interest might be charged to a foreigner, not to a brother, and obedience would bring blessing; the unto a stranger thou mayest lend upon usury (interest to a stranger allowed); the but unto thy brother thou shalt not lend upon usury (not to a brother); the that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all that thou settest thine hand to (the blessing on obedience); the figure (the distinction between the stranger and the brother, the charity owed to the brother; the truth that the foreigner, a trader, might be charged, but the needy brother helped freely; the figure of the blessing that follows charity and obedience, God blessing the open hand; the brotherly love that lends without gain, rewarded by God; the blessing on the obedient in the land, vv.19). The verse holds together the distinction and the blessing.

21:
"When thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee." The witnesses note the charge to pay a vow without delay, for God will require it — the crux of the sacredness of vows and the obligation of promises made to God. On "when thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee," interpreters expound the charge. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the obligation — "when thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee": a vow once made to God was to be paid without delay; the witnesses note the significance — the sacredness and binding force of vows made to God (a vow is a solemn promise to God, not to be neglected or delayed, God will require it); the lesson — that what is promised to God must be performed, the delay or breaking of a vow a sin (the vow voluntary, but once made, binding; the slackness in paying it a sin God will require); the truth that God holds men to their promises, "better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay," Ecclesiastes 5:5; "when thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it; for he hath no pleasure in fools," Ecclesiastes 5:4; the figure — the seriousness of all promises and commitments made to God, the call to faithfulness and integrity before him; the truth that the worship of God requires truth and performance, not empty words; the figure of the integrity that keeps its word to God, the vow a debt to be paid; the warning against the rashness that vows and the unfaithfulness that fails to pay; the call to weigh well what is vowed and then to perform it. The figure of the sacredness of vows and the obligation of promises to God.

On "when thou shalt vow a vow unto the LORD thy God, thou shalt not slack to pay it," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the charge. Keil and Ellicott note the vow once made to be paid without delay, the obligation binding (the performance required, Ecclesiastes 5:4) — the charge (slack not to pay).

On "for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee; and it would be sin in thee," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the requirement. Calvin and Gill note that God will surely require the vow, the failure to pay a sin, the integrity owed to God (the binding promise, Ecclesiastes 5:5) — the figure (the integrity that keeps its word to God); the providence (the charge, the freedom not to vow to follow, vv.22). The verse displays the charge to pay a vow without delay, for God will require it.

The witnesses agree the substance: a vow once made to God was to be paid without delay, for God will require it (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the sacredness of vows: pay thy vow, slack not — the binding force of a solemn promise to God, the vow voluntary but once made obligatory, the slackness or breaking of it a sin God will require (Ecclesiastes 5:4-5), a figure of the seriousness of all commitments to God and the integrity that keeps its word, the worship of God requiring truth and performance, not empty words. The verse displays the sacred-vow crux. The verse marks the vow: to be paid. The verse displays the delay: forbidden. The verse displays God: requiring it. The verse displays the integrity: owed. The charge displays the sacredness and binding force of vows to God. The verse displays the promise: binding. The verse displays the slackness: sin. The verse displays the word: kept. The charge to pay a vow without delay, for God will require it, displays the crux: thou shalt not slack to pay it: for the LORD thy God will surely require it of thee (Ecclesiastes 5:4-5) — a vow once made to God to be paid without delay, the binding force of a solemn promise, the failure to pay a sin God will require, the integrity that keeps its word to God. The verse displays the freedom not to vow, but the obligation once vowed (vv.22-23).
22:
"But if thou shalt forbear to vow, it shall be no sin in thee." The very brief verse notes that to forbear vowing is no sin. The grammatical voices note the freedom. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if thou shalt forbear to vow, it shall be no sin in thee" — vowing was voluntary, and not to vow was no sin; the but if thou shalt forbear to vow (the forbearing); the it shall be no sin in thee (no sin); the figure (the freedom not to vow, the vow voluntary; the truth that the vow was not commanded, but once made was binding; the figure of the liberty in things not commanded, the vow a free act; the contrast with the sin of breaking a vow, the safety of not vowing rashly; the freedom that warns against rash vows, vv.21). The verse holds together the freedom not to vow.

On "But if thou shalt forbear to vow," the witnesses note the forbearing. Gill notes the freedom to forbear vowing (the liberty) — the forbearing (no vow).

On "it shall be no sin in thee," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the no sin: "it shall be no sin in thee" — no sin in not vowing (the vow voluntary); the figure (the liberty in things not commanded); the providence (the freedom, the obligation once the word is spoken to follow, vv.23). The verse displays that to forbear vowing is no sin.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the freedom. The verse marks the vow: voluntary. The verse displays the forbearing: no sin. The verse displays the liberty: granted. The verse displays the obligation once the word is gone out of the lips (v.23).
23:
"That which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform; even a freewill offering, according as thou hast vowed unto the LORD thy God, which thou hast promised with thy mouth." The very brief verse notes that what is once vowed must be kept and performed. The grammatical voices note the obligation. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "that which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform... according as thou hast vowed unto the LORD thy God, which thou hast promised with thy mouth" — once the vow was spoken, it was binding and to be performed; the that which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform (the spoken vow kept); the even a freewill offering, according as thou hast vowed (the freewill vow binding); the which thou hast promised with thy mouth (the promise of the mouth); the figure (the binding force of the spoken vow, the word once given to be kept; the truth that the freewill offering, once vowed, became an obligation; the figure of the integrity that performs what the lips have promised, the word to God a debt; the spoken promise binding, the vow to be paid; the faithfulness that keeps the word given to God, vv.21). The verse holds together the binding spoken vow.

On "That which is gone out of thy lips thou shalt keep and perform," the witnesses note the spoken vow. Gill notes the spoken vow to be kept and performed (the obligation) — the spoken vow (kept).

On "even a freewill offering, according as thou hast vowed unto the LORD thy God, which thou hast promised with thy mouth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the promise: "according as thou hast vowed... which thou hast promised with thy mouth" — the freewill vow, once spoken, binding (the promise of the mouth); the figure (the integrity that performs the promise); the providence (the obligation, the law of eating in the neighbour's field to follow, vv.24-25). The verse displays that what is once vowed must be kept and performed.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the obligation. The verse marks the spoken vow: binding. The verse displays the word: kept. The verse displays the promise: performed. The verse displays the integrity: required. The obligation displays the binding force of the spoken vow. The verse displays the vow: kept. The verse displays the promise: a debt. The verse displays the faithfulness: shown. The verse displays the law of eating the neighbour's grapes and corn (vv.24-25).
24:
"When thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure; but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel." The very brief verse notes the liberty to eat the neighbour's grapes but not carry any away. The grammatical voices note the liberty. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure; but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel" — the passer-by might eat his fill of grapes but not carry any off; the when thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill (the eating allowed); the but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel (no carrying away); the figure (the liberty to satisfy hunger, but not to plunder, the balance of kindness and right; the truth that the law allowed the hungry to eat but not the greedy to take; the figure of the generosity that feeds the passer-by, yet guards the owner's property; the kindness that relieves want without licensing theft; the charity and restraint together, vv.25). The verse holds together the liberty to eat but not carry away.

On "When thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure," the witnesses note the eating. Gill notes the liberty to eat grapes one's fill (the kindness) — the eating (one's fill).

On "but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the restraint: "thou shalt not put any in thy vessel" — no carrying away (the owner's right guarded); the figure (kindness without licensing theft); the providence (the liberty, the same for the corn to follow, vv.25). The verse displays the liberty to eat the neighbour's grapes but not carry any away.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the liberty. The verse marks the grapes: eaten. The verse displays the hunger: relieved. The verse displays the vessel: not filled. The verse displays the property: guarded. The liberty displays the balance of kindness and right. The verse displays the eating: allowed. The verse displays the carrying: forbidden. The verse displays the charity: with restraint. The liberty to eat the neighbour's grapes but not carry any away displays the balance of kindness and right: thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure; but thou shalt not put any in thy vessel — the passer-by free to satisfy hunger but not to plunder, the generosity that feeds the hungry yet guards the owner's property. The verse displays the same liberty for the standing corn (v.25).
25:
"When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn." The very brief verse notes the liberty to pluck the neighbour's corn by hand but not reap it. The grammatical voices note the liberty. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn" — the passer-by might pluck ears of corn by hand but not use a sickle to reap; the when thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand (the plucking allowed); the but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn (no reaping); the figure (the liberty to satisfy hunger by hand, but not to harvest, the balance of kindness and right; the truth that the law allowed the hungry to pluck but not to plunder with a sickle; the figure of the generosity that feeds the passer-by, the kindness the disciples used, "his disciples were an hungred, and began to pluck the ears of corn," Matthew 12:1; the charity that relieves want without licensing theft; the kindness and restraint together, vv.24). The verse closes the chapter, the law of charity and restraint.

On "When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand," the witnesses note the plucking. Gill notes the liberty to pluck ears by hand (the kindness, Matthew 12:1) — the plucking (by hand).

The verse displays the liberty to pluck the neighbour's corn but not reap it.
